options.label: A terminal-based interface that allows using Kiro directly from the command line
A VS Code exclusive plugin that replaces the integrated terminal
A cloud computing service for running builds remotely
An application performance monitoring tool

questions.explanation: Kiro CLI is the terminal-based interface of Kiro, allowing developers to interact with the agent directly from the command line without needing an IDE.

questions.text: Kiro CLI's headless mode allows non-interactive execution, ideal for CI/CD pipelines.

options.label: True
False

questions.explanation: Kiro CLI's headless mode allows execution without human interaction, being ideal for automation in CI/CD pipelines where no interactive terminal is available.

questions.text: What is the function of custom agents in Kiro CLI?

options.label: Allow creating specialized agents with custom instructions and tools for specific tasks
Completely replace Kiro's default agent in all operations
Are used exclusively to manage repository access permissions
Function only as visual templates for the graphical interface

questions.explanation: Custom agents in Kiro CLI allow creating specialized agents with custom instructions and toolsets suited for specific tasks like code review, data migration, or security analysis.

questions.text: A team needs to integrate Kiro into their CI pipeline for automatic PR reviews. Which approach correctly uses the Agent Communication Protocol (ACP)?

options.label: Configure Kiro CLI with ACP, an open standard that enables compatible editors (JetBrains, Zed) to use Kiro as an AI agent backend
Install Kiro as a browser extension so reviewers can access it via web interface
Create a custom webhook that sends emails with review suggestions
Run Kiro manually on each developer's machine before opening the PR

questions.explanation: The Agent Client Protocol (ACP) is an open standard that enables Kiro CLI to work as an AI agent backend for compatible editors like JetBrains IDEs and Zed. In headless mode, it can also be used in CI/CD pipelines for task automation.

questions.text: What are the effort levels available in Kiro CLI?

options.label: Low, medium, high, xhigh, and max
Basic, standard, and premium - defining the user's subscription plan
Fast, normal, and slow - adjusting the agent's response speed
Single, batch, and parallel - determining how many files are processed simultaneously

questions.explanation: The effort levels (low, medium, high, xhigh, and max) in Kiro CLI control how much reasoning the model applies to prompts. Lower levels produce faster, shorter responses, while higher levels spend more tokens on deeper analysis and thorough code generation.

questions.text: Session management in Kiro CLI allows resuming previous conversations while maintaining the complete interaction context.

options.label: True
False

questions.explanation: Kiro CLI supports session management, allowing users to resume previous sessions with all context preserved, facilitating long-running work that spans multiple interactions.

questions.text: How does the Kiro CLI permission model work regarding tool access?

options.label: Custom agents declare tool categories (read, write, shell, web, spec) and permission rules inline in the markdown configuration file
All tools are available by default and the user blocks unwanted ones via .kiroignore
The administrator defines global permissions in the Kiro panel that apply to all agents
Permissions are inherited from the operating system and cannot be customized

questions.explanation: In Kiro CLI, custom agents declare tool categories with simple tags like read, write, shell in the markdown configuration file. Permission rules and MCP servers are embedded inline, and the file can be shared via version control.
